meta.title: 1.1 What Is a Webpage, Really?
meta.description: Discover what is behind every webpage by using View Source, and learn to read HTML tags as simple labels for content.

blocks.prompt: When you right-click a webpage and select "View Source," what do you think you will see? What do you think HTML looks like before the browser turns it into a visual page?

blocks.content:
## The simplest webpage in the world Here is a complete (tiny) webpage: ```html

Welcome to my site

This is a paragraph of text.

``` That is it. If you saved those two lines in a file and opened it in a browser, you would see a large bold heading that says "Welcome to my site" followed by a normal-sized paragraph below it. Let's break down what is happening. ## Tags are labels The words inside the angle brackets — `

`, `

` — are called **tags**. They are labels that tell the browser what kind of content follows: - `

` means "this is a top-level heading" - `

` means "this is a paragraph" - `

` means "this is a second-level heading" (like a subheading) You do not need to memorize a long list of tags. The important ones come up so often that you will recognize them naturally. For now, just remember: **a tag is a label that says what the content is.** ## Opening and closing tags Most tags come in pairs — an **opening tag** and a **closing tag**: ```html

This is a paragraph.

``` - `

` is the opening tag — it says "a paragraph starts here" - `

` is the closing tag — the `/` means "this paragraph ends here" - Everything between them is the content Forgetting the closing tag is one of the most common mistakes in HTML. If you see content that looks weirdly merged together or styled wrong, a missing closing tag is often the culprit. ## Nesting: tags inside tags Tags can go inside other tags. This is called **nesting**: ```html

Article Title

First paragraph of the article.

Second paragraph of the article.

``` The `
` (short for "division") is a container that wraps the heading and paragraphs. Think of it like a folder that groups related content. Nesting has one critical rule: **tags must close in the reverse order they opened.** Like nesting bowls — the last one you put in is the first one you take out. This is correct: ```html

This is bold text.

``` This is broken: ```html

This is bold

``` The second example closes `

` before closing ``, which is like trying to pull out the outer bowl before the inner one. Browsers will try to fix this silently, but the result is often unpredictable. ## Try it: View Source Right-click on any webpage and select **"View Page Source"** (or press `Ctrl+U` on Windows / `Cmd+Option+U` on Mac). What you see is the raw HTML that the browser received. It looks intimidating at first, but now you know the core idea: it is just content wrapped in labels. Look for tags you recognize — `

`, `

`, `

`. They are there, surrounded by plenty of other tags you do not know yet. That is fine. You are reading HTML.

blocks.question: On a scale of 1-5, how confident are you that you could look at a block of HTML and identify where one element starts and ends?
blocks.answer: If you rated yourself 3 or higher, you are on track. The core skill is spotting the opening tag, the closing tag (with the /), and the content between them. Everything else builds on that.

problems.question:
What would this HTML look like in a browser? ```html

Our Products

We sell handmade candles.

Best Sellers

```
options: A) A large heading "Our Products," a paragraph below it, then a slightly smaller heading "Best Sellers"
B) Three lines of text all the same size
C) A large heading "Our Products" with everything else inside it
problems.explanation:
Each tag defines a separate piece of content.

renders as a large heading,

renders as normal paragraph text, and

renders as a heading that is smaller than h1 but larger than regular text. They appear one after another, top to bottom.

problems.question:
You asked AI to generate a heading with bold text inside it. It produced this: ```html

Welcome to Our Store

``` The page looks fine in Chrome, but it is broken in another browser. What is the issue?
options: A) The <strong> tag is not allowed inside <h1>
B) The closing tags are in the wrong order — should come before
C) The <h1> tag should be <h2> instead
problems.explanation:
Tags must close in the reverse order they opened. Since was opened inside

, it must close before

closes. The correct version is:

Welcome to Our Store

. Browsers often try to silently fix this, which is why it might look fine in one browser but break in another.

problems.question:
A colleague says their landing page has a weird layout — the tagline and the description seem to be smashed together on one line instead of being separate. Here is the HTML: ```html

Fast. Simple. Reliable.

Our platform helps you ship faster.

``` What is most likely causing the problem?
options:
A) The

tag is missing its closing

tag

B) The

tag should be

instead

C) The text needs a
tag between the lines
problems.explanation:
Without a closing tag, the browser does not know where the heading ends. It may treat the paragraph as part of the heading, causing the text to merge. The fix is to add before the

tag:

Fast. Simple. Reliable.

.

blocks.prompt: In your own words, explain what HTML tags are and how opening and closing tags work. Use an analogy from your own experience if it helps.

blocks.content: HTML is content wrapped in tags. Tags are labels — they tell the browser what each piece of content is. Most tags come in opening/closing pairs, and they must nest properly (close in reverse order). When something looks wrong on a webpage, a missing or misordered closing tag is one of the first things to check.

blocks.prompt: Now you know what tags are and how they work. But a real webpage has more structure than just headings and paragraphs — it has a head, a body, navigation, and sections. Next, we will zoom out and look at the full anatomy of an HTML page.

reviewQuestions.question: HTML tags come in pairs. What is the correct pair for a paragraph?
reviewQuestions.explanation:
Most HTML tags have an opening tag (

) and a closing tag (

) with a forward slash. The content goes between them.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's the pattern! Opening tag, content, closing tag with a slash.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect:
HTML tags come in opening (

) and closing (

) pairs. The closing tag has a forward slash before the tag name.

reviewQuestions.question: What does HTML stand for and what does it do?
options: Hyper Text Markup Language — it labels content so the browser knows what each piece is
High Tech Modern Language — it creates interactive animations
Hyperlink and Text Management Library — it manages databases
reviewQuestions.explanation: HTML (HyperText Markup Language) wraps content in tags that tell the browser what each piece of content is — headings, paragraphs, links, etc.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Perfect! HTML is markup — it labels and structures content.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: HTML stands for HyperText Markup Language. Tags are labels that tell the browser what each piece of content is.
